359

We discussed – I have changed below to ensure it matches the core four points the 
PM wanted to make at PMQs in order to sound as active and confident as possible. 
Welcome views and amends to make the language more user friendly, but hopefully 
the substance makes sense. @Michael Ellam @Varun Chandra shout if anything 
goes further than you would be comfortable with on the talks.
DBT SoS statement – to be issued from Wednesday 12th March, 4am GMT
Business and Trade Secretary Jonathan Reynolds said:
“It's disappointing the US has today imposed global tariffs on steel and aluminium.
“We are taking a pragmatic approach and a trade war is in nobody’s interests
We are focused on rapidly negotiating a wider economic agreement with the US 
to eliminate additional tariffs and benefit UK business and our economy
Meanwhile we remain resolute in our support for UK industry. This Government is 
working with affected companies today, and I back industry’s application to the 
Trade Remedies Authority to investigate what further steps might be necessary to 
protect UK producers.
I will continue to engage closely and pragmatically with the US to press the case for 
UK business interests. We will keep all options on the table, and won’t hesitate to 
respond in the national interest.
ENDS
Are you going to retaliate?
The tariffs on steel and aluminium proposed by the Trump Administration are global, 
not targeted at the UK.
We are looking at all options, but we won’t be imposing tariffs immediately.
Let’s be clear. Industry doesn’t want to see a trade war where both sides keep 
escalating the situation. Standing up for industry means finding a solution.
Our approach to the US is pragmatic and we continue to have productive discussions 
on tariffs and other issues.
We have raised this with the US at various levels to make the case for UK companies 
to be exempt from US global tariffs and will continue to do so through our productive 
conversations.
You aren’t retaliating now but why aren’t you retaliating now? Other countries are.
• We are taking a pragmatic approach in seeking a wider economic deal. A trade war
is in nobody’s interests.
JCS
360


• We will keep all options on the table, but our focus is securing a deal that will benefit
industry and the British people.
Isn’t this a pretty weak response?
• I wouldn’t accept that – this is a cool-headed and pragmatic response.
• We rule nothing out and we will always act in the best interests of the British people
and economy.
• But a knee-jerk response today won’t help us achieve a long-term economic deal
with the US tomorrow, and that is our focus.
Why no exemption? PM failed at diplomacy?
We have raised this with the US at various levels to make the case for UK companies 
to be exempt from US global tariffs.
The Prime Minister has raised steel and aluminium tariffs with President Trump - in a 
call on Monday and when he was at the White House in February. The Business and 
Trade Secretary has also raised this with his counterpart Secretary Lutnick.
Our focus is on reaching a wider economic deal will benefit the industry and the 
British people.
Doesn’t say much for PM’s relationship with POTUS if they spoke and Trump ignored 
him?
• It is because of the Prime Minister’s successful visit to the White House that UK and
US officials are in talks about a future economic deal that will benefit businesses on
both sides of the Atlantic.
• These wider global tariffs have come into effect while we are in the middle of those
negotiations.
• We remain focused on that future trading partnership. We already have a fair and
balanced trade relationship and are the largest single investors in one another’s
economies, so there is plenty we can build on.
Is the industry also now going to be hit by tariffs from Trump?
The tariffs on steel, aluminium and derivatives being proposed by the Trump 
administration are global, not targeted at the UK.
We will continue to take a pragmatic approach and remain prepared to defend the 
UK's national interest where it is right to do so.
EU and Canada have announced retaliatory tariffs, weak PM?
I don’ t accept that. Our approach to the US has been pragmatic and we continue to 
have productive conversations with the US administration at all levels.
Our focus is a long-term wider economic deal which will see benefits for the industry 
361


and hardworking British people.
How will these damage the steel industry?
We have been working closely with the steel and aluminium sectors over the last few 
weeks to understand what the expected impact of tariffs will be on industry and will 
continue to do so.
In 2023, the US accounted for 5% of UK steel exports and 6% of UK aluminium 
exports.
Whilst these aren’ t huge volumes, that does not mean we are not taking this extremely 
seriously and doing everything we can to try to avoid UK industry being hit.
What are you doing to protect the steel industry?
This government will simply not allow the end of steel making in the UK.
That’ s why we have committed up to £2.5 billion of investment to rebuild the UK 
steel industry and support communities now and for generations to come. 
And next month, the final measure included in the British Industry Supercharger 
package comes into force, bringing energy costs for important UK industries –  
including steel –  closer in line with other major economies around the world.
Once fully implemented, the total value of reduced electricity prices is expected to be 
between £320 million and £410 million this year, and over £5 billion over 10 years.
We also back the industry’ s application to the Trade Remedies Authority (TRA) 
investigate what further steps might be necessary to protect UK producers.
If needed:
Only 5% of UK steel exports and 6% of aluminium exports (by volume) go to the US 
(worth noting it’ s high value, so a higher % by value).
The current steel safeguard is critical to protect our industry against unfair trading 
practices and was recently extended to June 2026.
This was designed to protect from the inflow of steel diverted from the US Market 
when it was put in place in 2018. Through the steel strategy, we will ensure that there 
is a plan for our critical steel industry following the expiration of the Safeguard.
How are we managing the risks to businesses in Northern Irelad?
We will always act in the best interests of all UK businesses which of course includes those 
in Northern Ireland.
As I have said, whilst we are not considering imposing tariffs immediately, we absolutely 
reserve the right to do so if necessary.
362


As per the Windsor Framework, where goods do not subsequently enter the EU, the Duty 
Reimbursement Scheme enables traders to reclaim or remit EU applicable duties in full 
without any limit on total claims.
We will look closely at any retaliatory tariffs announced by the EU any impact these might 
have on businesses, but it is our understanding these will not come into effect immediately 
[guide 31 March if public].
Will you revive UK tariffs on US products including whiskey, jeans and 
motorbikes?
All options are on the table so it’ s one option, but we won’ t be imposing tariffs 
immediately.
We’ re prioritising engagement with the US to stand up for industry and find a 
resolution. We will continue to take a cool-headed approach and reserve our right to 
retaliate, remaining prepared to defend the UK's national interest.
What engagement are you doing?
Industry Minister Sarah Jones met senior representatives from the steel and aluminium 
sectors yesterday and the Secretary of State speak to UK Steel today.
As you will have seen yesterday, the Trade and Economics policy manager at UK 
steel said that making the UK’ s steel exempt from the tariffs is a “ key priority”  and 
that DBT is “ all over it” .
Some steel companies have said you should make preparations to implement 
emergency tariffs should there be evidence of import surges in the coming 
months. Are you?
UK Steel has applied to the TRA for a functional review of the steel safeguard 
measure in light of the US tariffs, as well as broader challenges posed by global 
overcapacity in the steel market, and I [the Business and Trade Secretary] back that 
application.
Market reaction?
As you know we do not comment on markets.
How are we managing the risks to businesses in Northern Irelad?
We will always act in the best interests of all UK businesses which of course includes 
those in Northern Ireland.
As I have said, whilst we are not considering imposing tariffs immediately, we 
absolutely reserve the right to do so if necessary.
As per the Windsor Framework, where goods do not subsequently enter the EU, the 
Duty Reimbursement Scheme enables traders to reclaim or remit EU applicable duties 
363


in full without any limit on total claims.
We will look closely at any retaliatory tariffs announced by the EU any impact these 
might have on businesses, but it is our understanding these will not come into effect 
immediately [guide 31 March if public].
What trade protections are in place for the steel and aluminium industries?
The UK currently applies 44 trade remedy measures in addition to the globally applied 
steel safeguard measure.
We currently apply a global safeguard measure on 14 separate steel product 
categories, 17 separate anti-dumping or anti-subsidy measures on certain steel imports, 
and 2 measures on certain aluminium imports.  
The Trade Remedies Authority regularly discusses trade remedy measures with 
industry, and we encourage continued dialogue and any applications for new measures 
or the review of existing measures to ensure that UK businesses are protected from 
unfair foreign trade practices.
Next steps to securing a trade deal? Timelines?
Getting the right deal is the most important thing, but we are working at pace with the 
US to deliver this.
We are already having regular dialogue with each other.
Why are you waiting until Spring to publish the Steel Strategy when industry 
needs action now?
We have committed to publish Our Plan for Steel, on which we’re currently 
consulting, in the Spring. It will look at long-term issues facing the sector such as high 
electricity costs, unfair trading practices and scrap metal recycling.
We want to move swiftly, but in a way which addresses the longstanding issues the 
sector has faced for years and in line with other key workstreams such as developing 
the Industrial Strategy and Trade Strategy.
This is not easy, but we are determined to succeed.
OFFICIAL
OFFICIAL-SENSITIVE

The PM wants to be clear we have done everything possible and that we are negotiating a 
better deal with the US, but all options remain on the table. I have drafted the lines below for

US

" We are taking a pragmatic approach and a trade war is 
in nobody’s interests. The UK-US trade relationship is 
worth $400bn a year
" Building on this, we are in the middle of negotiations on 
an economic agreement to eliminate additional tariffs and 
benefit UK business and our economy
" But I will keep all options on the table, and won’t 
hesitate to respond in the national interest if we need to

Thanks Jason, a few suggestions below, and I agree if we can make the point we have a 
potential future deal on the table as a PM success that would be great, and I think fine to say 
tariffs would be part of that hopefully, but also don’t want to be a hostage to fortune and we 
generally do not talk about negotiations, but appreciate it’s a tricky one to answer.
OFFICIAL

Thanks @
 @Ailsa – there are three points where I would expect Ministers and the PM to be 
pushed beyond what we already have below:
• You aren’t retaliating now but why aren’t you retaliating now? Other countries
are.
We are taking a pragmatic approach in seeking a wider economic deal. A trade war is in nobody’s 
interests.
We will keep all options on the table, but our focus is securing a deal that will benefit industry and the 
British people.
• Isn’t this a pretty weak response?
I wouldn’t accept that. Other countries have also decided not to impose immediate retaliatory tariffs 
[WE THINK] and we want to focus on a long term economic deal.
We will always act in the best interests of the British people and economy.

• We’ve heard a lot about PM’s great relationship with POTUS – but they’ve
spoken and Trump still ignored him. Not great is it?
Can we make the argument that the PM succeeded in his White House visit in getting an economic 
deal on the table –  which we hope to exempt steel/aluminium when it comes to pass –  and these 
global tariffs have arrived as we work together towards that?
Would welcome wider thoughts.
JK

Updated script with DBT additions and bringing my last email up with DBT statement – welcome 
views:
DBT SoS statement – to be issued from Wednesday 12th March, 4am GMT
Business and Trade Secretary Jonathan Reynolds said:
“It's disappointing the US has today imposed global tariffs on steel and aluminium.
"The UK will always be a champion of free and open trade which is essential in 
delivering our Plan for Change.
“We are resolute in our support for UK industry. This Government is working with 
affected companies today, and I back industry’s application to the Trade Remedies 
Authority to investigate what further steps might be necessary to protect UK 
producers.
“Standing up for industry means finding solutions to the global challenges we face, 
and I will continue to engage closely and pragmatically with the US to press the case 
for UK business interests.”
ENDS
Also, I have looked into the Northern Ireland angle of this, and it is my understanding that 
any EU retaliatory tariffs announced tomorrow won’t come into effect until the end of March,

369


so we have a bit of time to work out a response. Essentially as it stands, NI businesses 
importing steel/aluminium from the US will have to take on the cost as per the Windsor 
Framework which sees the free movement of goods between NI and Ireland –  do correct me 
if I am wrong here.
I think the below line works but let me know:
How are we managing the risks to businesses in Northern Irelad?
We will always act in the best interests of all UK businesses which of course includes those 
in Northern Ireland.
As I have said, whilst we are not considering imposing tariffs immediately, we absolutely 
reserve the right to do so if necessary.
As per the Windsor Framework, where goods do not subsequently enter the EU, the Duty 
Reimbursement Scheme enables traders to reclaim or remit EU applicable duties in full 
without any limit on total claims.
We will look closely at any retaliatory tariffs announced by the EU any impact these might 
have on businesses, but it is our understanding these will not come into effect immediately 
[guide 31 March if public].
US TARIFFS 20250312
ISSUE: The US global tariffs on steel and aluminium have come into effect.
RESPONSE:
It is disappointing to see the US has imposed global tariffs on steel and aluminium.
We absolutely reserve the right to impose retaliatory tariffs, and we will continue to 
have productive conversations with the US administration at all levels on this issue.
But our focus is on reaching a wider economic deal will benefit the industry and the 
British people.
Any action we take will be in the interest of UK businesses, consumers and the 
economy.
Response to tariffs?
The tariffs on steel and aluminium proposed by the Trump Administration are global, 
not targeted at the UK.
We are looking at all options, but we won’ t be imposing tariffs immediately.
Let’ s be clear. Industry doesn’ t want to see a trade war where both sides keep 
escalating the situation. Standing up for industry means finding a solution.
Our approach to the US is pragmatic and we continue to have productive discussions 
370


on tariffs and other issues.
We have raised this with the US at various levels to make the case for UK companies 
to be exempt from US global tariffs and will continue to do so through our productive 
conversations.
Why no exemption? PM failed at diplomacy?
We have raised this with the US at various levels to make the case for UK companies 
to be exempt from US global tariffs.
The Prime Minister has raised steel and aluminium tariffs with President Trump - in a 
call on Monday and when he was at the White House in February. The Business and 
Trade Secretary has also raised this with his counterpart Secretary Lutnick.
Our focus is on reaching a wider economic deal will benefit the industry and the 
British people.
Is the industry also now going to be hit by tariffs from Trump?
The tariffs on steel, aluminium and derivatives being proposed by the Trump 
administration are global, not targeted at the UK.
We will continue to take a pragmatic approach and remain prepared to defend the 
UK's national interest where it is right to do so.
For latest news and information from Downing Street visit: http://www.gov.uk/number10
Follow Number 10 on X (Twitter): http://x.com/10DowningStreet
